name: Valley of the Kings
shortDescription: A royal burial ground on the West Bank of Luxor, Egypt, where pharaohs of the New Kingdom were interred.
description: The Valley of the Kings, located on the West Bank of Luxor (ancient Thebes), served as the principal burial site for the pharaohs of the New Kingdom (c. 1550-1070 BCE). Carved into the Theban hills, these tombs were designed to protect the pharaohs' mummified remains and funerary goods, ensuring their successful passage into the afterlife. The valley's remote location was chosen to deter tomb robbers, though most tombs were eventually plundered. The valley is divided into the East Valley, where most royal tombs are located, and the West Valley, containing fewer, less significant tombs. The discovery of Tutankhamun's relatively intact tomb in 1922 by Howard Carter brought immense fame to the valley and fueled public fascination with ancient Egypt. The Valley of the Kings remains a significant archaeological site, providing invaluable insights into the beliefs, rituals, and artistic achievements of ancient Egyptian civilization. The tombs were not only burial chambers but also elaborate representations of the pharaoh's journey through the underworld, with walls adorned with scenes from the Book of the Dead and other religious texts.
subtitle: Resting place of pharaohs, gateway to eternity.
location: West Bank of Luxor (ancient Thebes), Egypt
significance: Royal burial ground; symbolic gateway to the afterlife

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Valley of the Kings' origins are closely tied to the religious beliefs of the New Kingdom Egyptians, particularly their emphasis on the afterlife. The pharaohs, considered divine rulers, required elaborate preparations for their journey to the realm of Osiris. The valley's seclusion offered a measure of protection against tomb robbers, a constant threat. The selection of this location likely held symbolic significance, possibly associated with the setting sun and the western direction, which represented the land of the dead in Egyptian cosmology. The mythology surrounding the tombs involves the pharaoh's transformation into a god, their passage through the underworld (Duat), and their eventual resurrection. The Book of the Dead, often inscribed on the tomb walls, served as a guide for the deceased pharaoh, providing spells and incantations to overcome obstacles and navigate the dangers of the underworld.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Valley of the Kings served as the primary burial site for pharaohs, queens, and some high-ranking nobles during the New Kingdom period. Its role was to ensure the safe passage of the deceased ruler into the afterlife, providing them with the necessary provisions, spells, and protection. The tombs were not merely burial chambers but also elaborate temples dedicated to the deceased pharaoh and their connection to the gods. The valley's significance lies in its reflection of ancient Egyptian beliefs about death, resurrection, and the divine nature of the pharaoh. The elaborate decorations and funerary goods found within the tombs provide invaluable insights into ancient Egyptian art, religion, and social structure. The valley acted as a sacred space where the mortal and divine realms intersected, allowing the pharaoh to continue their reign in the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Valley of the Kings is rich in symbolism. The location itself, on the west bank of the Nile, is associated with the setting sun and the land of the dead. The hidden nature of the tombs symbolizes the mysteries of the afterlife and the pharaoh's secret journey to immortality. The elaborate decorations, including scenes from the Book of the Dead, represent the pharaoh's trials and tribulations in the underworld. The placement of funerary goods, such as food, clothing, and weapons, symbolizes the pharaoh's continued existence and power in the afterlife. The tombs can be interpreted as microcosms of the Egyptian universe, reflecting the order and balance that the pharaoh maintained during their reign. The valley also symbolizes the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, a central theme in ancient Egyptian religion.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Valley of the Kings has had a profound and lasting legacy on modern culture. The discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb in 1922 sparked a global fascination with ancient Egypt, influencing art, literature, film, and popular culture. The valley continues to be a major tourist destination, attracting visitors from around the world who come to marvel at the grandeur of the pharaohs' tombs. Archaeological research in the valley continues to uncover new tombs and artifacts, shedding further light on ancient Egyptian civilization. The Valley of the Kings has also inspired countless works of fiction, from adventure novels to historical dramas, cementing its place in the popular imagination. Its influence can be seen in architectural styles, design motifs, and even fashion trends, demonstrating the enduring appeal of ancient Egyptian culture.

sources.author: Nicholas Reeves
sources.work: The Complete Valley of the Kings
sources.description: Comprehensive guide to the history, archaeology, and significance of the Valley of the Kings.

sources.author: Kent R. Weeks
sources.description: Detailed exploration of the tombs, their construction, and the lives of the pharaohs buried there.

associations.significance: The Valley of the Kings is directly linked to the Egyptian concept of the afterlife.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: A symbol of immortality and divinity, often found in the tombs.

worshipPractices.description: Elaborate ceremonies performed to ensure the pharaoh's successful transition to the afterlife.

worshipPractices.description: Food, drink, and other goods were offered to the pharaoh in their tomb.

festivals.name: Beautiful Feast of the Valley
festivals.description: A festival where statues of Amun were brought to the West Bank, including the mortuary temples, to visit the deceased.

cultural.modernLegacy: A major archaeological site and tourist destination, inspiring fascination with ancient Egypt.
